key_matchups: Edson Álvarez vs Czechia's ball progression in the midfield turnover leading to Mexico's chance
Ladislav Krejčí vs the opening physicality, including the early head injury stoppage
phases.summary: The opening phase is defined by an open, lively tempo rather than clearly described tactical structures. Czechia looked the more threatening side early, with a couple of shooting chances, while Mexico had a major opportunity after Edson Álvarez won the ball and Romo found space to shoot. The text suggests both teams are settling in with direct chances and little evidence yet of major shape changes or block shifts.

adjustments: tempo slowed after the hydration break
both sides struggled to create chances through the middle and in transition
key_matchups: Denis Višinský vs Mexico's defensive line/box defenders
Adam Hložek vs Mexico's aerial defending on wide service
Israel Reyes vs Czechia's failed clearance on the acrobatic effort
Gilberto Mora and Roberto Alvarado combining against Czechia's midfield/defensive pressure
Julián Quiñones vs Czechia's challenge in a dangerous area
phases.summary: After an early period of relative parity, the match settled into a slower, scrappier phase following the hydration break, with both teams finding it difficult to generate sustained threat. Czechia briefly looked more dangerous through Denis Višinský, while Mexico’s best moments came from a few isolated combinations and half-chances rather than any sustained tactical edge.

formations_blocks: cagey, low-tempo first-half setup from both sides
adjustments: no changes at half-time; both teams restart with the same personnel
phases.summary: The first half was extremely cautious, with both teams seemingly prioritizing structure over risk and producing only a modest combined xG of 0.46. There were no substitutions or shape changes at the break, so the second half begins with both sides unchanged and still needing more urgency, especially Czechia.

formations_blocks: Mexico probing with long balls from the back
Mexico recycling possession and playing through midfield
Czechia pushing forward after falling behind
Czechia attacking with crosses/services into the box
adjustments: Mexico shifted from direct long balls to more effective midfield recycling
Mexico attacked Czechia down the left-hand side repeatedly
Czechia increased urgency and pushed for a response after conceding
Czechia committed more numbers forward in search of a goal
key_matchups: Mateo Chávez vs Czechia back line on the breakaway goal
Jorge Sánchez vs Czechia left-side defensive line on the second goal
Julián Quiñones vs Czechia defense in and around the box
Luis Romo delivering into the box with no target
phases.summary: Mexico began the phase by testing Czechia with direct balls from deep, but found more success once they recycled possession and played through midfield. The breakthrough came from transition, with Mateo Chávez finishing a breakaway, before Czechia were punished again down their left side as Julián Quiñones cleaned up for 2-0. After that, Czechia pushed higher and tried to force a response, but their final delivery into the box remained poor and ineffective.

formations_blocks: training-exercise / audacious, low-consequence open play in stoppage time
adjustments: Tomáš Souček was forced off injured at 85' and replaced by Alexandr Sojka
key_matchups: Tomáš Souček vs Guillermo Ochoa (Souček closing down Ochoa before going down injured)
phases.summary: Mexico saw out the closing stages by monopolizing possession, with the crowd even turning to celebratory “ole” chants as Czechia’s late efforts to surge forward repeatedly came to nothing. The phase was punctuated by Ochoa’s emotional late introduction and Souček’s non-contact injury, before Mexico added a stoppage-time third from broken play to complete a comfortable 3-0 win.
